topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The doorbell rang, a sharp, insistent sound that made me jump. I peered through the peephole, my heart giving a sudden, unwelcome thump against my ribs. A package sat on the mat, clearly addressed to the previous occupant, Sarah Jenkins. My hands started to sweat, and I instinctively smoothed down my shirt, a nervous habit I couldn't seem to break. Who would be sending something to me? I fumbled with the lock, my fingers clumsy with apprehension. When I finally pulled the door open, the box was there—a plain brown cardboard rectangle tied with twine. The handwriting on the label was familiar, and a wave of awkwardness washed over me. I felt every eye in the hallway, even though I was alone.

[story 2]

I found the package tucked behind the recycling bin. It was addressed to the unit above mine, clearly marked "Tenant: J. Smith." My stomach tightened instantly. I picked it up, the cardboard feeling flimsy and alien in my grip. I retreated to my apartment, pacing the narrow living room. Every few seconds, I’d glance toward the door, half-expecting someone to materialize. I tried to focus on rearranging the books on the shelf, but the movement felt forced, jerky. I kept running my thumb over the rough texture of the packing tape, a futile attempt to ground myself. It was just a box, nothing more, but it felt like a spotlight had suddenly been shone directly onto my recent tenancy.

[story 3]

When the delivery person handed me the parcel, the sheer formality of the exchange made me acutely aware of my own awkwardness. I accepted the box with a stiff nod, my gaze fixed somewhere near the floorboards. It was clearly meant for someone else, someone who had already moved out. The weight of the package in my hands seemed disproportionate to its size, and I worried about how I was holding it—too tightly, perhaps. I walked back to my door, my steps hesitant, as if navigating a minefield. Inside, I set it down on the counter, the sound of the tape tearing faintly echoing in the quiet space. I stood there for a long moment, just breathing, trying to appear completely unbothered while my entire frame felt rigid with unspoken scrutiny.
